name: Tsukuyomi
description: Tsukuyomi, also known as Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto, is the moon god in Japanese mythology and the Shinto religion. Born from Izanagi, the creator god, as he washed his right eye after escaping the underworld, Yomi, Tsukuyomi is considered one of the three noble children, alongside Amaterasu (the sun goddess) and Susanoo (the storm god). While his gender is sometimes debated, he is generally depicted as male. Tsukuyomi is associated with the moon, night, and time. His role in the myths is less prominent than Amaterasu and Susanoo, and his personality is often perceived as aloof and enigmatic. A key myth involving Tsukuyomi describes his anger and subsequent slaying of the food goddess Uke Mochi after she produced food from her nose, mouth, and anus. Amaterasu, disgusted by this act, refused to ever face Tsukuyomi again, leading to the separation of day and night. Though less detailed than other deities, Tsukuyomi remains an important figure representing the lunar cycle and the passage of time, a constant presence illuminating the night sky. His influence is felt in various Shinto rituals and beliefs, connecting him to cycles of growth and decay.
subtitle: God of the Moon and Night

keyMyths.title: Birth of Tsukuyomi
keyMyths.description: Tsukuyomi was born from Izanagi's right eye after he purified himself from his journey to the underworld. This made him one of the three noble children.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Uke Mochi
keyMyths.description: Tsukuyomi killed the food goddess Uke Mochi after being disgusted by her method of producing food. This act angered Amaterasu.

keyMyths.title: Separation of Day and Night
keyMyths.description: After Tsukuyomi slew Uke Mochi, Amaterasu refused to ever face him again, resulting in the separation of day and night. They inhabit separate realms.
